title: Re-defining Humor Data Objects for AI Humor Research
research_questions: How can a humor data point be redefined to represent humor as a social interaction rather than as a binary text label?
Can an LLM generate a structured receiver explanation R for why humor succeeds or fails given context C, humorous attempt X, and observed reaction Y?
Does a revised prompt that explicitly assesses text sufficiency and data quality reduce errors in LLM-generated humor explanations?
What failure modes arise when generating humor explanations from transcripts of multimodal sources such as TED Talks or sitcoms?

methods_proposed.name: Humor reasoning data object (C, X; Y, R; Z)
methods_proposed.summary: The paper defines a humor data object as a quintuple consisting of social context C, teller humorous attempt X, receiver reaction Y, receiver explanation R, and teller recovery response Z after humor failure. This reframes humor data around teller and receiver roles rather than only a binary humor label.

methods_proposed.name: Text-sufficiency-aware humor explanation prompt
methods_proposed.summary: The revised prompt asks the LLM to decide whether the transcript alone supports a humor explanation, flag missing context, multimodal dependence, transcription issues, and alignment problems, and then output reasoning steps, a reconstructive flow chart, a one-sentence explanation, and a hallucination flag.

humor_theories.detail: Martin and Ford's definition of humor as social behavior motivates modeling teller, receiver, social context, reaction, explanation, and recovery in the proposed data object.

Suls (1972) and humor theory/social cognition motivate making the appreciation process explicit through step-by-step reasoning and flow charts; Prompt 2 asks for setup or expectation, trigger phrase, intended association/reference, and shift/incongruity.

evaluation_methods.detail: The authors manually reviewed outputs on the same 31 examples for Prompt 1 and Prompt 2, judging each output as acceptable or unacceptable based on whether it matched their interpretation of the transcript.

Before scaling generation, the authors ran a small pilot and manually inspected whether outputs were valid JSON, whether the reasoning remained solid based on the transcript, and whether the model avoided hallucinated humor explanations.

headline_results.finding: On the manually reviewed set, Prompt 1 produced 17 acceptable outputs and 14 incorrect outputs.
Prompt 1's 14 incorrect outputs were categorized as incomplete context, named-entity explanation failures, and multimodality failures.
headline_results.value: 7 incomplete context; 2 named entities not being properly explained; 5 multimodality

headline_results.finding: On the same 31 examples, Prompt 2 produced 27 correctly labeled outputs and 4 errors.
The remaining Prompt 2 errors consisted of one missing-context case and three likely multimodal-dependence cases.
Using the revised prompt, the authors produced a scaled generated dataset of humor explanations.

key_findings: The paper proposes representing humor as a social interaction with context, humorous attempt, reaction, explanation, and recovery rather than as only a binary label.
Manual error analysis showed that many poor explanations came from incomplete context, unexplained named entities, multimodal dependence, and unreliable transcript alignment or wording.
The revised prompt improved manual performance on the same 31 examples from 17 / 31 acceptable outputs to 27 / 31 correctly labeled outputs without changing the base model.
Prompt 2 was more cautious about forcing explanations when the transcript was insufficient and added labels and flags for text support and data quality.
The authors generated 307 explanation examples with the revised pipeline, presenting them as a foundation for future humor data synthesis, data augmentation, and humor understanding work.
Automatic prompt optimization using DSPy/GEPA did not improve performance beyond manual prompt adjustment in the authors' setup.
limitations: The preliminary work focused primarily on R, the receiver explanation; Z, the teller's recovery response after humor failure, remains future work.
Most evaluation was manual review on a small subset of 31 examples.
The generated 307-example dataset is not described as a final gold-standard resource and needs more systematic evaluation.
Many source examples come from multimodal settings, so transcripts may omit visual cues, timing, delivery, gestures, images, or earlier setup needed to explain laughter.
Prompt 2 still made 4 errors on the 31-example set: 1 missing-context case and 3 likely multimodal-dependence cases.
The authors have not yet shown whether the generated explanations improve downstream humor tasks such as humor detection, humor generation, or appropriateness modeling.
safety_ethics_notes: The paper notes that future work should distinguish what is funny from what is socially appropriate, and should study socially appropriate recovery strategies such as clarification, deflection, or apology after failed humor.

human_samples.population: Speakers/performers in existing SMILE (TED+sitcoms) source material whose transcript utterances provide context C and candidate humorous attempt X.
human_samples.observations: Transcript utterances were used to construct local textual context C and punchline/utterance X; the paper reports 31 manually reviewed examples and 307 generated explanation examples, but not the number of unique speakers/performers.
human_samples.detail: The paper uses existing datasets with TED Talks and sitcom examples; it does not report source-speaker counts, selection criteria, or demographic details.

human_samples.population: Audiences in TED Talks or sitcoms represented in SMILE source examples.
human_samples.observations: Observed audience reaction Y was represented as laughter/no laughter or humor succeeded/failed; no audience-member count is reported.
human_samples.detail: Y is described as the receiver reaction: 1 means audience laughed/felt humorous and 0 otherwise. The current paper reuses these observable reactions rather than recruiting audiences.

human_samples.population: Paper authors/research team performing manual review.
human_samples.observations: 31 generated explanations were double-checked one-by-one for Prompt 1; the same 31 examples were tested under Prompt 2; a small pilot before scaling was manually inspected, but its size is not reported.
human_samples.detail: The paper says 'we manually reviewed,' 'we double-checked,' and 'we judged each output as acceptable or unacceptable based on whether it matched our interpretation of the transcript.' It does not report the number of reviewers or whether review was independent.

judges.detail: Manual author/research-team judgment determined the reported Prompt 1 vs Prompt 2 results on the 31-example set: Prompt 1 had 17 acceptable and 14 incorrect outputs; Prompt 2 had 27 correctly labeled outputs and 4 errors. Blinding is not reported.

judges.name: SMILE/TED+sitcom audience reactions
judges.detail: Audience laughter/no-laughter in the existing source material determined the reaction label Y used as input to explanation generation.

judges.name: gpt-4o-mini
judges.detail: The LLM generated explanations and, under Prompt 2, also generated text_support_label, data_quality_flags, reasoning_steps, flow_chart, final_explanation, and hallucination_flag for the resulting data objects. These labels/metadata are model outputs, not independent human annotations.

generation_selection.scope: Prompt 1 explanation generation on 31 manually reviewed examples
generation_selection.detail: The paper reports 31 outputs for 31 examples under Prompt 1 and does not report multiple candidates, reranking, or best-of-N selection.

generation_selection.scope: Prompt 2 explanation generation on the same 31 manually reviewed examples
generation_selection.detail: The paper reports 31 outputs for the same 31 examples under Prompt 2 and does not report multiple candidates, reranking, or best-of-N selection.

generation_selection.n: 1 final generated explanation example per produced data object; 307 explanation examples total
generation_selection.detail: After an unquantified pilot, the authors used the revised prompt to produce 307 explanation examples. No candidate sampling/selection procedure is reported.
The paper says DSPy/GEPA was explored and did not improve performance beyond manual prompt adjustment, but gives no generation, candidate, selection, or evaluation details.

model_provenance.name: gpt-4o-mini
model_provenance.detail: The paper states: 'All explanations were generated using the same base LLM checkpoint: [gpt-4o-mini].' It does not report API/provider details, exact snapshot, release variant, or inference dates.

inference_budgets.scope: Manual evaluation generation for Prompt 1 and Prompt 2 on 31 examples
inference_budgets.candidates_per_item: 1 reported output per example for each prompt
inference_budgets.detail: The paper says both prompts were applied to the same 31 examples. It does not report API-call counts, retries, parsing failures, random seeds, temperature, top_p, max tokens, latency, compute, or cost.

inference_budgets.scope: Scaled revised-prompt generation of 307 explanation examples
inference_budgets.candidates_per_item: 307 final explanation examples produced; number of candidates or retries per source item not reported
inference_budgets.detail: The paper reports a small pilot followed by a larger run yielding 307 examples, but does not report pilot size or generation budget details.
No optimization budget, number of trials, objective, validation split, model calls, or compute/cost information is reported.

human_baselines.scope: Humor explanation generation task R given C, X, and Y
human_baselines.detail: The paper does not evaluate human-written explanations against LLM-generated explanations under matched conditions. Humans served as manual judges/error analysts, not as a baseline system.

contamination_risk.basis: The 31-example evaluation and 307-example generation are based on an existing public source dataset, SMILE (TED+sitcoms), cited as 2023, and on TED/sitcom transcript material that may be present in proprietary LLM training data. The model is gpt-4o-mini, but the paper provides no training-data disclosure, held-out/private item construction, or decontamination test. Manual review checks output quality but does not mitigate possible source memorization or prior exposure.

llm_judge_dependence.affected_claims: The generated dataset's per-instance text-support, data-quality, and hallucination labels are produced by gpt-4o-mini; the main Prompt 1 vs Prompt 2 performance table is based on author manual judgment rather than LLM-as-judge evaluation.
llm_judge_dependence.human_validation: Manual validation is reported for the same 31 examples and an unspecified small pilot; the full 307-example generated dataset is not systematically human-validated in the paper.
llm_judge_dependence.detail: The paper explicitly proposes future LLM-as-a-judge evaluation only after validating a rubric against human judgments. In the reported study, the LLM is primarily the generator/data-labeler, while authors are the primary evaluators.

reporting_gaps: Number, identity, expertise, and demographics of manual reviewers are not reported.
Blinding, independent annotation, adjudication procedure, and inter-annotator agreement are not reported.
Sampling/selection method for the 31 manually reviewed examples is not reported, so representativeness is unclear.
The size and composition of the initial source pool from SMILE, source split between TED and sitcoms, Y=1/Y=0 distribution, and number of unique speakers/audiences are not reported.
Pilot size before the 307-example scaled run and precise go/no-go criteria are not reported.
The 307 generated explanation examples are not systematically evaluated; the paper reports only preliminary/manual inspection and future evaluation plans.
Model access route, provider/API endpoint, exact snapshot/version beyond 'gpt-4o-mini,' and inference dates are not reported.
Sampling and decoding parameters such as temperature, top_p, max tokens, seed, stop conditions, retry/JSON-repair policy, and parsing failure rate are not reported.
Compute, latency, and monetary cost are not reported.
DSPy/GEPA prompt-optimization details, including objective, training/validation split, number of trials, and evaluation method, are not reported.
No contamination or decontamination analysis is reported for SMILE, TED Talk transcripts, sitcom transcripts, or gpt-4o-mini.
No directly comparable human baseline with human-authored humor explanations is included.
